item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't see really how this is impressive. From what I gather, it's some sort of attempt at trying to be a meta-example at tackling SCP-166 by taking it in the usual dark direction and so on.

Honestly, I just got the vibe of it being angry ragey fanfiction. Honestly, it does also reflect certain phenomenon and traits within the SCP community so I suppose that's something worthwhile. Even if that reflection isn't good.

I guess it's pretty well-written and mildly fascinating, but it just comes off as mean-spirited. Past the well done prose and format, what is this exactly?

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I believe UE has stated that that's intentional on their part, because 1465 was originally intended to be a rewrite of 105.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
noticed a mistake in the links to other SCPs, the SCP-105 sends you to SCP-1465 and vice-versa.

Bizarre. Maybe poke the author about this?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not gonna lie, the first time I read the article I thought it was the real SCP-166. And when I wanted to re-read it I went to the real SCP-166 instead of this one. Btw, I noticed a mistake in the links to other SCPs, the SCP-105 sends you to SCP-1465 and vice-versa.

Asides from that, you got my upvote. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 0166 is such a dark tale. It takes the least generous reading of the original 166 article and runs with it to make a bitter revenge story. After reading I felt like I had taken a class F anti nostalgia amnestic. What ever I used to feel about scp 166 is forgotten. It takes significant effort to remember why I still like it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm having a hard time phrasing what I felt about this piece, but it feels like a necessary and humane treatment of this character. I've at times taken long breaks from the SCP fandom due to encountering a tale or article which took a far too nonchalant, in my opinion, approach to the subject of rape or pedophilia, though thankfully not for a few years now, as the general tone of the writing here has matured. I just appreciated the agency granted to the character, and the feeling, not of overdramatic emotion, despite her gothic trappings, but just …tiredness, which I found realistic and surprisingly moving. Good work.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One small nitpick:

spoilers
– hide block

played into her demonic origins

Succubi are technically, devils, not demons. Devils are lawful evil (control the universe, judgment, etc) while demons are chaotic evil (destroy the universe, undo the natural order, etc).

Otherwise as good a response as any I've probably ever read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Any way we can nuke the original 166 off the site and replace it with this one?

Unless the original drops low enough in rating to merit a rewrite or the author decides to rewrite the piece, no. But if you're intent on this, that would be a matter to take up with the Rewrite Team.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Any way we can nuke the original 166 off the site and replace it with this one? I mean that only half-jokingly. This is much better than it in every way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is genuinely fantastic. The original 166 is just… unthinkably gross, and I think this is a great answer to it that really plays well off of the original article's flaws. Realizing that this wasn't, in fact, the new holder of the 166 slot was a massive disappointment.

item.title: Re: Novote
item.content:encoded:
Okay, I see. That's a lore bit deserving of criticism, absolutely.
That makes sense that you would hate this trope, it is kinda dumb.

Now that I understand the age thing, I would probably change my rating to +1.

item.title: Re: Novote
item.content:encoded: It's a lore thing. I personally hate the "since this page doesn't change that means the time never happens" trope.

item.title: Novote
item.content:encoded:
For some reason, I feel compelled to comment on this article.

This being a metatextual exploration of 166 canon (if that word is appropriate here) is fascinating to me. It is very helpful for understanding what problems people have with 166, as I don't really have any - perhaps I am not mature enough to find the piece largely problematic. The article explores (as I interpret it) the development of wiki's standards and can be used as an essay on that, of sorts. These things are useful to me as a concept reviewer (there was even an idea of 166 rewrite couple of weeks ago) and as a relatively new member of the wiki.

However, there is a thing that rubs me absolutely the wrong way, perhaps due to the lack of understanding of pataphysics. I can't understand what is up with 166's age. When she complains about being stuck at 16 is that a part of her lore I am forgetting or is it a metatextual thing? Because if it the former, I am sorry for my ignorance, but if it the latter, I don't really feel like it should work like that. While she is 16 in the context of the original article, I really don't feel like that implies her not getting older 'behind the scenes'. And as things are capable of changing inside the story as this tale shows, they should be capable of changing in between stories. I don't know how to put it in words, but I feel like it is wrong or I am missing something.

I would also don't really agree with an opinion in the comments that this should replace the SCP-166 article because I think that would ruin the metatextuality of it, a bit. As in, the original need to be present for this tale to make sense.

Disclaimer: no expertise in literature, arts or philosophy, so I am more than capable of being completely wrong. Feel free to point that out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Having her be raped to build character development is, in my opinion, 100x grosser than anything in the original article. That, the way that she's blatantly stating the author's opinions in a way that's completely out-of-character, and my general dislike of "pataphysics" = -1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Huh. Took a minute to understand what this was about (as I haven't really dealt with much patatphysics) but once I did, oh man this is good. Not surprised at all with the direction the narrative took.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Re: the blank silence, that was intentional. The other stuff, not so much, but you bring up a good point with the first.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I can't say I totally understood everything this story was doing, but what I can do is say how it made me feel. It put into words everything I thought about the original but couldn't articulate. Like having my discomfort validated.

That said, I spotted some things that might be mistakes. I obviously can't tell if they were intentional though given the meta nature of the story.

+ show block
– hide block

unfurnished Type-2 Disposable humanoid containment unit

I got stuck on this sentence, rereading it because I kept reading it as "disposable-humanoid". Intentionally ambiguous wording? The paragraph later talks about recycling the units, but the character can also be interpreted as feeling like trash. This is least likely to be a real mistake, so I am only mentioning it for completeness and since it was so early in the article that I couldn't pick up a potential double meaning yet.

PoI-000 proceeded towards SCP-166-ARC's containment, which they proceeded to breach

Double "proceeded" seems awkward.

(    second silence)

Third interview log has lots of numberless pauses

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a common enough criticism that I have seen it twice in the span of a couple days. At that point, I feel the need to address the following:

I fundamentally disagree with it
for the following reasons.

The original 166 was a reasonable-in universe response to the existence of an entity with the traits 166 displayed,

There's points to be made about the diegetic implications of a piece, and how that can break something in two. Such points, however, must concede that they are extensions of the author's will. Anything put to paper was put there through the direction of a creator.

SCP-166 was not merely an in-universe response: it was also the event it responded to. It is not so much the interpretation of an existent entity, because that entity does not exist without it. The crux of critique can therefore be laid on both 166 and its containment with equal measure.

this however comes across as someone having interpreted the original article in a radically different way,

The author is, metaphorically, dead. Intent, therefore, is secondary to the story told by the actual narrative and its techniques.

The purpose of SCP-0166 was twofold:

  1. To explore the ramifications of SCP-166's situation, and how she might react in the face of a shitty existence that reads like a hentai to many.
  2. To challenge the idea of the pure story, e.g. that any work on the wiki is simply a recount of "how something happened", no matter how much it skews into narrative memeplexes.

In this case, you're right: I am interpreting it radically differently. That is not inherently bad: compare Stanley Kubrick's the Shining to Steven King's miniseries, along with each's reception. Perhaps, for an example closer to home, compare SCP-847classic with SCP-847: when one gets down to it, the latter is really just an expanded version of the former, but it rose to the hundreds while its predecessor got wiped.

then putting their response to that interpretation in the SCP's mouth.

SCP-166 is not real. She is an idea conveyed through words, itself conveyed through written language, to be interpreted by the reader. There is no canon.

And really, is it such a shock that little miss "I get to choose between forcible nudity and cellulitis!" would be miffed to learn she was a character archetype?

Essentially, this has moved 166 from shallow but still sympathetic to yet an other thing that kills people for no other reason than it wants to,

SCP-0166 is not the villain of this piece, I am. I chose to create a situation where the consequences of her anomaly were explored to a depressing conclusion. I chose the setting that allowed every misfortune to compound. I, as much as I criticized it, played into her demonic origins while retaining the psyche that suffered for it. Most importantly, I am responsible for her ultimate tragedy: that she is still a trope, albeit a less creepy one, doomed to play a depressing role, never to realize why she will never be happy. That is, of course, taking into account the fact that she does not exist in a physical sense.

She was never meant to be "dangerous", or even an antagonist. She is the bright orange neon sign, headlined by a giant fuck-off Garfield to say something that, in post, is obscured by a shitty mspaint "ART IS AN EXTENSION OF THE ARTIST".

Of course she has a reason: I have willed the idea of her self-awareness, I have willed her distaste for her previous story, and I have willed her the desire to break her previous story in two.

promptly justifying every precaution the Foundation ever took in universe.

In the context of the story, I find this bit irrelevant, because the precautions were similarly irrelevant. 0166 only really breaks after A) a breach of an unrelated precaution and B) extended bureaucratic hand-wringing.

Just an other evil in a box we're supposed to pity but can't, because it rejects empathy via its own characterization.

Empathy is not an on-off switch. If someone tells you not to pity them, you don't suddenly stop pitying them, because pity is not conditional on whether or not someone wants pity. Pity is primarily a feeling in response to events that have happened to someone.

Unless I'm reading this wrong.

tl;dr: This is not a story about a monster, so much as one about the necessary limitations of narrative.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The original 166 was a reasonable-in universe response to the existence of an entity with the traits 166 displayed, this however comes across as someone having interpreted the original article in a radically different way, then putting their response to that interpretation in the SCP's mouth.
Essentially, this has moved 166 from shallow but still sympathetic to yet an other thing that kills people for no other reason than it wants to, promptly justifying every precaution the Foundation ever took in universe.
Just an other evil in a box we're supposed to pity but can't, because it rejects empathy via its own characterization.
-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I definitely enjoyed this more than the original, wall-breaks and shots at the original and all. I also how like how this article calls BS on the Foundations 'sympathetic' image. This rewrite has certainly done SCP-166 a favor.

I wonder what Series 1 article is next?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have two main issues with this.

+ show block
– hide block

The first is that I've always been iffy about the use of 'Pataphysics as a synonym for Metafiction, since that wasn't its original intended meaning within the SCP-verse. Rimple, The Great Hippo, and a few other authors have constructed specific narratives regarding the true fictional/metafictional nature of 'pataphysics, which I feel this sort of thing — despite being an excellent metafictional anomaly in its own right — dilutes slightly. But in the interest of breaking out the high-energy Therisno Cannons, I'm not gonna let that minor gripe stand in the way of an upvote.

The second is something I've been wavering about, and is why I originally no-voted: because this is 'pataphysical/metafictional/metaphysical/'patafictional/whatever, the antagonist here is the author of the original SCP-166, or at least their fictional analogue. This isn't a defence of the original by any means; it's just that this blurs the line between in-universe fiction and out-of-universe fiction in a way that makes me a little bit uncomfortable. But, then again, I suppose that's the point.

Regardless, those things aren't enough to stop me from thoroughly enjoying it. It's certainly better than the original :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was quite disappointed to figure out that the old article hadn't actually been Arc'd and this isn't the new official holder of the SCP-166 slot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Completely and utterly brilliant, probably one of the best articles I've read in a short while. +1'd

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Amazing work, I can't believe you managed to turn this skip around so well!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The original version always bothered me.

Your version advances the narrative and critique the original brilliantly.

It’s honest in my top five list of favorite SCP articles.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I appreciate the characterization and dialogue generally, but I'm really over pataphysics-adjacent concepts. Please don't take the downvote as a judgment on how you've constructed this story, I'm just not into it as a story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Your approach to prose and dialogue baffles me to this day, uranium. Awesome stuff, this was goddamn fantastic.
An excellent take, UE.

item.title: +1, +1, a thousand times +1
item.content:encoded:
This was so incredibly necessary, it's maddening that it hadn't been done sooner. The dialogue is very well crafted, and the emotion felt and expressed by 166 is very VERY complex and yet present. We feel for her, even though she's really mean and unhelpful.

Extremely well done, and no, I swear I'm not biased just because I hate the original with every bone in my body.

(or maybe I am, who knows?)

item.title: author pox
item.content:encoded:
you took what mattered, what mattered the most
AND THIS IS ME TAKING IT BACK

A while ago, I got the phrase "I was a teenage succubus" stuck in my head. Before that, I didn't like SCP-166. I guess this is killing two birds with one stone, then.

Credit to TheMightyMcB, Laneous, and Henzoid for their crit.
